claims.claim_text: A November 2023 GKE e-commerce incident scaled from 3,000 to 45,000 RPS, hit a 15,000-connection Cloud SQL limit, caused a 28-minute outage, and lost $2.3 million.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In November 2023, an e-commerce platform ran their product catalog API on GKE. Every product page required three database queries: product details, pricing, and reviews. During a flash sale, traffic jumped from 3,000 to 45,000 requests per second. Cloud SQL hit its connection limit at 15,000 connections. The auto-scaler was adding pods, but each new pod opened more database connections, making the problem worse. The site went down for 28 minutes during peak sale time. Estimated lost revenue: $2.3 million.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A flash-sale traffic spike can overwhelm a relational database when each request fans out into multiple queries and every newly scaled pod opens more database connections.
claims.rationale: This is a precise outage narrative with named platform, date, timings, and financial impact but no attributable real incident source.

claims.claim_text: In the same incident, a self-managed 3-node Redis StatefulSet silently evicted 60% of the cache 20 minutes before the sale, leaving the database to absorb the full 45,000 RPS.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The team had a Redis cluster running in GKE -- but it was a self-managed StatefulSet with 3 nodes and no monitoring. They did not discover until the postmortem that Redis had silently evicted 60% of the cache 20 minutes before the sale started due to a memory limit that nobody had reviewed since initial deployment. The database was hit with the full 45,000 RPS because the cache was effectively empty.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A self-managed cache with weak observability and poorly reviewed memory settings can silently evict data before peak traffic, pushing the full read load back onto the database.
claims.rationale: These are incident-specific operational facts with exact counts and timing but no attributable postmortem.

claims.claim_text: After migrating to Memorystore, the next sale handled 62,000 RPS while the database saw only 800 QPS for a 98% cache hit rate.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: They migrated to Google Memorystore for Redis with proper memory sizing, connection limits, and eviction monitoring. The next sale handled 62,000 RPS with the database seeing only 800 QPS -- a 98% cache hit rate. Caching is not optional for production Kubernetes workloads. It is the difference between your database being a bottleneck and your database being a safety net.
claims.suggested_rewrite: After moving to a managed Redis service with deliberate sizing, connection limits, and eviction monitoring, teams can dramatically reduce database load during later traffic spikes.
claims.rationale: This keeps the operational lesson while removing unsupported exact outcome numbers from the same unattributed incident.

claims.claim_text: Redis supports richer data structures and built-in messaging/scripting features than a simple key-value cache, including strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams, pub/sub, and scripting.
claims.span_hint: section: Redis vs Memcached, decision matrix rows Data structures / Pub/Sub / Lua scripting
claims.rationale: The Redis upstream repository overview explicitly describes Redis data structures and messaging/scripting capabilities.

claims.claim_text: Redis offers optional persistence through RDB snapshots and AOF.
claims.span_hint: section: Redis vs Memcached, decision matrix row Persistence
claims.rationale: The official Redis persistence documentation is the right primary source, but redis.io is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Memcached is a multithreaded key/value cache store.
claims.span_hint: section: Redis vs Memcached, decision matrix rows Strings only / Multi-threaded
claims.rationale: The upstream Memcached repository describes Memcached as a multithreaded key/value cache store.

claims.claim_text: Memcached relies on client-side distribution logic and does not provide built-in replication or persistence in the way the table implies.
claims.span_hint: section: Redis vs Memcached, decision matrix rows Replication / Clustering / Persistence
claims.rationale: The Memcached documentation is the correct primary source for these design properties, but docs.memcached.org is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The max item size row gives exact 512 MB and 1 MB limits.
claims.span_hint: section: Redis vs Memcached, decision matrix row Max item size
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Max item size | Supports much larger single values by default | Smaller default item limit |
claims.rationale: This cross-engine exact-number comparison is unnecessarily brittle for an instructional matrix and can be rewritten to preserve the practical distinction.

claims.claim_text: The threading row states that Redis is single-threaded with I/O threads since 6.0 while Memcached is multithreaded.
claims.span_hint: section: Redis vs Memcached, decision matrix row Multi-threaded
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Multi-threaded | Mostly single-threaded command execution | Multi-threaded |
claims.rationale: The row mixes a version-specific detail with a broader architectural contrast; the rewrite keeps the usable distinction without the fragile version anchor.

claims.claim_text: For 90% of Kubernetes workloads, Redis is the right choice.
claims.span_hint: section: Redis vs Memcached, paragraph after decision matrix
claims.anchor_text: **For 90% of Kubernetes workloads, Redis is the right choice.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **For many Kubernetes workloads, Redis is the more versatile choice.**
claims.rationale: The exact percentage is unsupported and not necessary to teach the recommendation.

claims.claim_text: The managed-service comparison table gives exact maximum-memory numbers for AWS, GCP, and Azure.
claims.span_hint: section: Managed Service Comparison, row Max memory
claims.anchor_text: | Max memory | 6.1 TB (cluster mode) | 300 GB (standard) | 1.2 TB (Enterprise) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Max memory | Depends on node type and cluster shape | Depends on product and deployment model | Depends on tier and current Azure Redis offering |
claims.rationale: These exact cross-vendor numbers age badly and mix different products and sizing models.

claims.claim_text: The cluster-mode comparison row says GCP cluster mode is available since 2024 and compares exact cluster support across all three vendors.
claims.span_hint: section: Managed Service Comparison, row Cluster mode
claims.anchor_text: | Cluster mode | Yes (up to 500 shards) | Yes (since 2024) | Yes (Premium/Enterprise) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Cluster mode | Yes | Available through Memorystore for Redis Cluster | Tier-dependent; verify the current Azure Redis product and tier |
claims.rationale: The GCP date is wrong and the row conflates separate products and tier models across providers.

claims.claim_text: A hot key can overwhelm a single shard because each Redis key belongs to one shard and Redis command execution is single-threaded enough for that shard to become CPU-bound.
claims.span_hint: section: Diagnosing Hot Key Distribution, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The official Redis monitoring guidance directly explains hot keys concentrating load on one shard, but redis.io is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `redis-cli --hotkeys` only works when the maxmemory policy is an LFU policy.
claims.span_hint: section: Diagnosing Hot Key Distribution, diagnosis item 2
claims.rationale: The official Redis CLI documentation states that `--hotkeys` only works when the maxmemory policy is `*lfu`, but redis.io is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `OBJECT FREQ <key>` is only available when the maxmemory policy is one of the LFU policies.
claims.span_hint: section: Diagnosing Hot Key Distribution, diagnosis item 3
claims.rationale: The official Redis command reference directly documents this requirement, but redis.io is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Running `MONITOR` in production can drastically reduce Redis performance.
claims.span_hint: section: Diagnosing Hot Key Distribution, diagnosis item 3
claims.rationale: The official Redis `MONITOR` page includes benchmark evidence for substantial throughput reduction, but redis.io is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The 65,000-connection figures in the connection-budget example align with AWS's documented per-node ElastiCache connection limit.
claims.span_hint: section: Connection Budget, table
claims.rationale: AWS documents a hard limit of up to 65,000 simultaneous connections per ElastiCache node.

claims.claim_text: Envoy's HTTP cache filter respects `Cache-Control` and only caches responses when it has enough information to calculate freshness lifetime.
claims.span_hint: section: Envoy Sidecar Caching, paragraph before Flask example
claims.rationale: The Envoy cache-filter documentation explicitly describes freshness calculation and `Cache-Control` behavior.

claims.claim_text: Redis processes over 100,000 operations per second on a single core in typical workloads, and the bullet explains that with in-memory operation and I/O threading since version 6.0.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Redis processes over 100,000 operations per second on a single core** in typical workloads. Despite being single-threaded for command execution, Redis achieves this throughput because it operates entirely in memory and uses efficient data structures like skip lists and hash tables. Since version 6.0, I/O threading handles network reads/writes on separate threads while command execution remains single-threaded.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Redis can process very high request rates from memory.** Actual throughput depends heavily on command mix, data size, client behavior, hardware, and benchmark setup.
claims.rationale: The specific throughput figure and the explanatory stack are bundled into a benchmark-style claim that is too exact for an uncited teaching aside.

claims.claim_text: The cache-stampede bullet says the topic was formally studied in a 2009 paper and that the technique is now standard practice at companies like Facebook.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.anchor_text: 2. **The term "cache stampede" was formally studied in a 2009 paper** titled "Optimal Probabilistic Cache Stampede Prevention" by Vattani, Chierichetti, and Lowenstein. The "probabilistic early expiration" technique from that paper is now standard practice at companies like Facebook, where cache stampedes on popular content could otherwise bring down entire database clusters.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **Probabilistic early expiration is a documented cache-stampede prevention technique** described in the literature on preventing multiple clients from regenerating the same expired item at once.
claims.rationale: The year is wrong and the named-company adoption claim is unsupported in the module.

claims.claim_text: AWS ElastiCache Serverless launched in 2023, auto-scales Redis, bills per ECPU and GB of storage, and can cut costs by 40-60% for variable traffic.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **AWS ElastiCache Serverless (launched 2023) automatically scales Redis** with no capacity planning required. It charges per ECPU (ElastiCache Compute Unit) and per GB of storage, eliminating the need to choose instance types. For workloads with variable traffic, this can reduce costs by 40-60% compared to provisioned instances.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **AWS ElastiCache Serverless automatically scales Redis workloads** and bills separately for stored data and request processing. For variable traffic patterns, it can sometimes be more cost-efficient than provisioned capacity.
claims.rationale: The launch timing and pricing model are real, but the exact 40-60% savings number is scenario-dependent and better treated as an illustration.

claims.claim_text: Google Memorystore for Redis Cluster became GA in 2024, supports up to 25 shards with 250 GB total capacity, and existed after a five-year gap.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Google Memorystore for Redis Cluster** became generally available in 2024, supporting up to 25 shards with 250 GB total capacity. Before this, GCP customers who needed Redis clustering had to self-manage Redis on GKE or use third-party services -- a gap that existed for over five years.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Google offers Memorystore for Redis Cluster as a managed clustered Redis service** for workloads that need sharding and replica support. Check the current product limits because they have expanded since launch.
claims.rationale: The GA date and capacity limits are outdated, so the specific historical framing should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Redis's default memory policy is `noeviction`, which returns errors when the instance is full.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row Ignoring memory eviction policy
claims.rationale: The official Redis eviction reference directly covers `noeviction`, but redis.io is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Storing serialized objects larger than 100 KB causes latency spikes.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row Storing serialized objects larger than 100 KB
claims.anchor_text: | Storing serialized objects larger than 100 KB | Convenient to cache entire API responses | Cache individual fields or use Redis hashes; large values cause latency spikes |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Storing very large serialized objects | Convenient to cache entire API responses | Cache individual fields or use Redis hashes; oversized values can increase latency and memory pressure |
claims.rationale: The 100 KB threshold is an uncited hard cutoff that is better expressed as a qualitative warning.

further_reading.title: Managing Clusters in ElastiCache
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers shard limits, replication-group structure, and how AWS models clustered Redis deployments.

further_reading.title: Memorystore for Redis Tier Capabilities
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the Basic and Standard tier behavior, read replicas, and failover model for non-cluster Memorystore.

further_reading.title: Memorystore for Redis Cluster Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows the separate clustered Redis product on Google Cloud and its shard-and-replica architecture.

further_reading.title: Envoy Cache Filter
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents the HTTP cache filter behavior, including cacheability and freshness semantics.

notes: Quiz prompts, quiz answers, exercise steps, Mermaid diagrams, and code blocks were excluded per pipeline rules. Several core Redis engine facts in this module point to redis.io, which is off the current allowlist and therefore marked `needs_allowlist_expansion`. Microsoft Learn now flags Azure Cache for Redis for retirement, so the Azure portions of this module may need a broader content refresh beyond citation injection.
